Chapter ends with Ivy realizing she can't fix this and the original message will never make sense. **Key beats:** Emma tracked down → Emma furious and refuses to explain → revelation partial and unsatisfying → apology rejected → ambiguity about what the text actually meant → Ivy stuck with guilt --- ## Chapter 7: The Aftermath Doesn't Get Better **~1000 words** Two weeks pass. Ivy tries to apologize to Jordan, catches them in the corridor. Jordan is cold. "I don't know what your problem is, but I don't need your concern. Please leave me alone." The apology lands nowhere. Jordan has decided Ivy is weird and untethered themselves socially to avoid association. At school, Ivy has become "that girl"—not popular, not unpopular, just *off*. People know her name for the wrong reason. Social media memes about "detective Ivy" die down but don't disappear entirely. Every few weeks someone resurfaces the screenshots, makes a new joke. Some people think she's cool for causing drama; most think she's pathetic. There's no consensus, which is worse. Sasha, who enjoyed the chaos, has moved on to other interests and become slightly embarrassed by the association. Marcus never fully re-engages with Ivy; they're cordial but the closeness is gone. He's disappointed in her and it shows in small ways. Ivy's parents are concerned and awkward about it. Her mum keeps asking "is everything okay at school?" Her mum's friend mentioned it at a dinner party. Ivy's life has reshaped around this one mistake. She's more anxious now, not less. She overthinks every social interaction. She doesn't trust her own judgment. She's not better for it. There's no resolution, no lesson integrated, no growth that makes it okay. There's just the weight of having fucked up and having to sit with it. Chapter ends with Ivy at a party in late autumn, overhearing two people she doesn't know discussing the "drama thing" from earlier in the year. They think it's funny. She doesn't. She leaves early. **Key beats:** Apology rejected → social position altered → memes persist → friendships fractured → new anxiety replaces old → no resolution → no redemption → ongoing discomfort --- ## Chapter 8: November **~950 words** It's been three months. The school year has moved on; new dramas have emerged; the attention faded but didn't vanish. Ivy is changed but not in a good way. She's more cautious, more self-conscious, more isolated. She hasn't received another mysterious text. She sometimes wonders what happened to Emma, whether Emma is okay, but they don't speak. She and Marcus are friends again but it's different—there's a permanent awareness of how Ivy spiraled and what he thought of her during it. Sasha still says hi but they run in different circles now. Jordan ignores her if they pass in corridors. Ivy has become someone known for creating chaos, for being unreliable in her judgment. She's written essays about it, thought about it obsessively, tried to understand what she did wrong. The understanding doesn't change the fact that it happened or that people remember. One day in November, a friend mentions another friend received a weird text from a number they didn't recognize. Ivy feels the familiar panic start to rise—what if, what if, what if. She opens her mouth to tell the friend to be careful, to investigate, to figure out who sent it. Then she stops. She tells the friend to block the number. The friend laughs—"It's probably just spam"—and the moment passes. But Ivy sits with it. She's learned something (maybe), but it's not a clean lesson. It's just learned caution, learned weariness, learned that her instincts aren't to be trusted. She's not healed or redeemed. She's just slightly more broken in a different way. The final image: Ivy, alone in the library,
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Ivy Chen **Background & Personality:** Ivy comes from a comfortable, anxious middle-class household. Her mother is a GP who is calm in crises but neurotic about everything else (keeps a spreadsheet of Ivy's exam dates); her father is a software engineer who retreats into logic when emotions get complicated. Only child. She's been "the serious one" since childhood—the kid who read mystery novels at seven, who kept a diary about potential problems, who raises her hand in class even when she's not entirely sure. She's clever but not exceptionally so; her intelligence lives in pattern-finding and overthinking rather than raw intellect. She has a rigid internal framework for how the world works—she believes good intentions matter, that investigating problems is the same as solving them, that anxiety is information rather than distortion. She's rigidly anxious in a very specific way: she doesn't panic about normal things (exams, social performance) but becomes *fixated* on small ambiguous signals. A text can spiral her for weeks. A teacher's tone can mean something. She's been in therapy once (Year 9, for "general anxiety") but found it frustrating because the therapist kept asking "what evidence is there" and Ivy realized her evidence was feelings, which felt disqualifying. She hasn't been back. Her main flaw: she cannot sit with ambiguity. She *must* solve the puzzle, even if solving it means creating a narrative that harms others. She's genuinely trying to help, which makes it worse—she doesn't register that she's causing damage until it's too late. **Physicality:** Tall and slightly awkward, like she hasn't quite settled into her body. Tends to hunch when thinking—shoulders forward, head down. Dresses practically (jeans, oversized hoodies, school uniform worn slightly too loosely). Doesn't really do makeup. Has dark hair she usually pulls back badly. Fidgets constantly: picks at her nails, bounces her leg, taps her pen. Speaks in rushes when anxious—sentences tumble out faster than she can edit them. Makes consistent eye contact even when uncomfortable, which reads as intense rather than friendly. Rarely smiles naturally; her default face is slightly worried. **Motives & Relationships:** Ivy *wants to be good*. More than anything, she wants to be the person who helps, who figures things out, who prevents disaster. She's terrified of missing something that matters. This is why she can't let the mystery go. Marcus is her oldest friend (since Year 7)—she loves him but increasingly resents that he doesn't take things seriously. She tells herself he's just privileged enough not to worry, but really she's hurt that he questioned her judgment. Sasha is her theatre friend—they've known each other for two years. Ivy likes Sasha's energy and wishes she could be more like her. She misses the signals that Sasha is enjoying the situation for entertainment rather than genuine concern. Jordan doesn't exist to Ivy as a real person until chapter 3. Then Jordan becomes a puzzle to solve, not a human to consider. **Voice Sample - Internal:** *The question mark though. You don't send a question mark unless you're trying to communicate something specific. Like they wanted her to know they got the message. Like they were testing if she was really paying attention. Three dots, then a question mark. That's not random. She pulled up the message again. The blood was so red. So intentional.* **Voice Sample - Dialogue:** "I'm not being insane. I'm being careful. Which is apparently insane now, apparently everything that isn't ignoring obvious signs of crisis is insane—" [stops, frustrated] "You don't understand because nothing bad has ever actually happened to you." --- # Marcus Wong **Background & Personality:** Marcus is the son of two lawyers who are very competent and very absent. His older sister is at Cambridge studying medicine (excellent, no pressure there). He's functionally independent—makes his own dinner, manages his own schedule, doesn't really report to parents. He's developed a coping strategy of treating serious things with humor and assuming most crises are overblown. This isn't callousness; it's survival. If he takes everything seriously, he'll crack under the weight of his parents' expectations and his sister's perfection. He's genuinely kind but covers it with sarcasm. He worries about Ivy *constantly* but masks it as skepticism. He's self-aware enough to know he uses humor as deflection but not enough to actually change it. He's dealing with low-level depression he hasn't named and won't admit to. His main flaw: he assumes intelligence = good judgment, and when Ivy stops thinking clearly, he pulls away rather than supporting her. He's judgmental in a way he'd never admit. He thinks people who spiral are weak. **Physicality:** Mixed race (Chinese mother, white British father), medium height, puts effort into his appearance in a way that looks effortless. Wears good trainers and band t-shirts that are actually bands he likes (not ironic). Good posture—stands with confidence even when he's anxious. Moves through space efficiently. Very expressive face; his eyes are readable even when he's joking. Has a nervous laugh he uses when uncomfortable. Keeps things light through physicality—touches people's arms when joking, creates space between himself and intensity. **Motives & Relationships:** Marcus *wants to not care too much about things*. He's cultivated an identity as the person who doesn't spiral, who keeps perspective, who can make a joke of anything. Ivy's breakdown threatens this identity because it shows him that even smart people can lose it. He's been friends with Ivy since Year 7 because she was the only person who didn't find his jokes exhausting—she got them. He's always had feelings for her that he's never fully examined. This makes her panic both more frustrating (she's spiraling) and more painful (he can't fix it). His parents barely notice him. His sister's success is suffocating. Ivy's crisis makes him feel like he has to be the stable one, which is exhausting. **Voice Sample - Internal:** *She's smart though, that's the thing. Ivy's genuinely clever. So how is she not seeing that she's basically invented a mystery and then decided to investigate it? It's circular. But saying that will make her worse. Better to just... not engage with the madness. Don't feed it. That's probably best.* **Voice Sample - Dialogue:** "Mate, you've got a text from an unknown number. That happens like fifty times a day. It's spam. Or a wrong number. Or literally anything except whatever crime scene you're currently writing in your head." --- # Sasha Williams **Background & Personality:** Sasha's parents are both in theatre (one's a stage manager, one's a set designer). She's grown up around performance and drama—literally and metaphorically. She's good at theatre genuinely, but also uses performance to avoid having a self. She shifts between personas depending on context and has lost track of which one is real, if any of them are. She's chronically bored by ordinary life. She needs external stimulation, novelty, drama. Not because she's malicious but because without it, she starts to collapse into anxiety herself. Her family is artsy and liberal but also chaotic—her parents are supportive but also somewhat checked out. No one's really monitoring what she's doing. She loves Ivy genuinely *and* she's using Ivy's crisis as entertainment. Both things are true. Her main flaw: she's a parasite on other people's intensity. She can't generate her own emotional content, so she feeds on others'. **Physicality:** Dyed hair (currently purple, will be something else in two months). Vintage clothes from charity shops, worn deliberately—everything is a costume. Makeup is theatrical even at school (eyeliner dramatic, colours intentional). Very physical with people—touches them, leans in, takes up space. Expressive hands when she talks. Beautiful in an affected way. Carries herself like she's perpetually on stage. Speaks in layers—joke, subtext, joke again. **Motives & Relationships:** Sasha *wants to matter*. She wants to be the center of something, to have narrative weight. When Ivy's mystery appears, it's like a gift—a real crisis she can be instrumental in solving, a plot she can inhabit. She loves Ivy mostly genuinely, but there's a layer of Ivy-as-vehicle beneath the love. When things get uncomfortable, Sasha will abandon Ivy because she's always able to re-enter the mainstream. Her parents would say she's fine. She's not fine. She's performing fine so well that everyone, including herself, has started to believe it. **Voice Sample - Internal:** *Oh my god, this is actually the most interesting thing that's happened all term. Like, actually a real mystery, a real person in crisis, and Ivy's going to help them and we're going to solve it. This is like a proper drama. This is better than drama. This is real.* **Voice Sample - Dialogue:** "We have to help them. Imagine if we just ignored this and something actually happened. We'd know we could have prevented it. That's the actual ethical choice—to investigate." --- # Jordan Cooper **Background & Personality:** Jordan is 17, Year 12, quiet by default rather than by choice. Their parents' divorce last year cracked something open in them. They're not depressed exactly, but they've lost the ability to perform normalcy consistently. Some days they're functional; some days everything feels pointless. They're dealing with it alone because asking for help feels like admitting something is wrong. They're genuinely a kind person, which makes it worse when people assume things about them. They read too much, have a dry sense of humor, and keep a very small circle of trusted people (Casey, and maybe two others). They're terrified of being known/exposed. School is increasingly exhausting. Their main flaw: when cornered, they become mean. Not maliciously—it's a defense mechanism. If you invade their privacy, they'll tell the world something about you that makes you regret it. **Physicality:** Androgynous, tall, thin—looks like their body is still catching up to their height. Dresses in blacks and greys, lots of layers. Doesn't make eye contact particularly; looks at people's mouths or shoulders instead. Moves quietly, takes up minimal space. Cries easily but tries not to. Fidgets differently than Ivy—more internal (tight jaw, balled fists in pockets). Their resting face reads as sad even when they're fine. **Motives & Relationships:** Jordan *wants to be left alone*. They're not trying to hurt anyone; they're just trying to survive the school day. They've built a protective shell and Ivy's "concern" feels like an attack on that shell. Casey is their anchor—the only person whose presence doesn't feel performative. When Ivy approaches Jordan, Jordan immediately tells Casey because they're panicking and Casey is safety. Jordan decides to retaliate against Ivy not from malice but from self-protection. If people are going to have theories about them anyway, they might as well point attention elsewhere. **Voice Sample - Internal:** *She's asking if I'm okay. Why is she asking me that? What did she hear? Who told her something? I need to find out what people are saying. I need to know. This is bad. This is how it starts—people getting concerned, people paying attention, people knowing things about you that you didn't tell them.* **Voice Sample - Dialogue:** "Did someone tell you something? What's this about?" [pause, when Ivy tries to explain] "No. Actually no. You need to stop. Whatever you think you know, you're wrong." --- # Emma McAllister **Background & Personality:** Emma is 15, Year 10, and has had a rough year. Her best friend moved schools; she's struggling with her body and sexuality and doesn't have language for any of it; she's been having dark thoughts that she doesn't fully understand. She's not diagnosed with anything but something isn't quite right and she knows it. The text she sent—the blood emojis—was a joke to close friends about her period starting at school, but it was also genuinely a moment of crisis. Both things. She was joking to make it bearable and genuinely panicking underneath the joke. The distinction matters to her and she will never explain it to anyone because it's mortifying and also too real. She's small and appears younger than she is. She has social anxiety but masks it with humor and a group of close friends who enable her. She's smart but scattered. Her main flaw: she's selfish in the way fifteen-year-olds can be—she hasn't yet developed the empathy to understand how her privacy being exposed affects her, versus how her *shame* about the privacy affects her. **Physicality:** Short, round, dyed her hair black recently (rebellion against her very blonde mother). Wears oversized jumpers and refuses to participate in PE if possible. Moves quickly, speaks quickly, like she's constantly trying to get ahead of discomfort. Very young-looking face. Rarely sits still. Bites her nails. **Motives & Relationships:** Emma *wants the text to disappear and for no one to know anything about her body or her struggles*. When Ivy approaches her, Emma's rage is partly at Ivy and partly at herself for sending something so revealing. She will not forgive Ivy. Not because Ivy is evil but because Emma needs someone to blame for her humiliation and Ivy is available. It's not fair to Ivy, but fairness isn't the point. Her close friends know about the text and the struggles, but the school doesn't. Ivy's investigation made that private shame semi-public, and Emma will never recover that privacy. **Voice Sample - Internal:** *I can't believe I sent that. I can't believe she has it. I can't believe she's using it to talk to me about my mental health like she's some kind of expert. Like she cares. She's just collecting other people's crises like they're interesting.* **Voice Sample - Dialogue:** "I don't want to talk about it. It doesn't matter what it was. You made it everyone's business and that's worse than whatever was actually happening." --- # Mr. Patterson **Background & Personality:** Mr. Patterson is early 40s, teaches English, married with two kids, trying to be a good teacher in a school system that's underfunded and overwhelming. He's well-intentioned and this is precisely the problem—good intentions without structural thinking. He hears rumors about Jordan and decides to intervene because that's what good teachers do. He doesn't realize that taking rumors seriously and acting on them is its own form of harm. He's overextended (teaching five classes, marking, pastoral duties) and working from pattern recognition. Bad situation + quiet student = concern. Activate protocols. It feels like helping. His main flaw: he mistakes action for help. He thinks doing something is always better than doing nothing. **Physicality:** Tired. Dresses in the slightly rumpled way of someone who had kids and stopped caring about appearance. Brown hair going grey. Wears glasses that he forgets he's wearing. Moves slowly, like everything requires effort. Smiles but it doesn't quite reach his eyes. Speaks carefully, like he's always considering the right thing to say. **Motives & Relationships:** Mr. Patterson *wants to save students*. He has one student from several years ago who he thinks about—someone he didn't help, who struggled, who dropped out. He's trying to not repeat that mistake. He doesn't know the cost of his help. He's not really in relationship with any of these students—they're abstractions to him, pastoral concerns. This distance allows him to act. **Voice Sample - Internal:** *The girl said Jordan might be struggling. I should check in. That's my job, that's what I'm here for. Just a casual conversation, just making sure they know adults are available. That's good teaching.* **Voice Sample - Dialogue:** "I just want to make sure you're okay. Sometimes students go through things and think they have to handle it alone. I'm here if you need someone to talk to."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1: 🩸🩸🩸 The notification came at 12:47 AM. Ivy saw it because she wasn't asleep—she was in that particular Friday-night state of being awake without reason, scrolling through her phone in bed, not looking at anything specific. The screen lit her face in the dark. Three blood drops. From a number she didn't recognize. She stared at it. 🩸🩸🩸 That was the entire message. Just the three emojis, no text, no context, no explanation. She read it again. Still three blood drops. She opened the chat thread—nothing else, just those three dots hanging in the void at 12:47 AM from someone called +44 7xxx 555 xxx. She sat up. Her first thought was that it was spam. Her second thought was that it was a wrong number. Her third thought was that it meant something specific and terrible and that she needed to take it seriously. She texted back: "???" Waited. Nothing came back. She watched the screen. One minute. Two minutes. The message sat there, her question mark hanging unanswered. She deleted it and typed again: "are you ok" and then stared at it. *Are you ok* sounded dramatic. It sounded like she was taking this seriously when it was probably nothing, probably just someone's phone glitching or a spam bot or something stupid. She deleted it. Then she typed it again anyway because what if it wasn't nothing? What if someone was actually in crisis and she'd ignored it? She sent: "are you ok" And waited. Three minutes. No response. The message had been read (had it? sometimes you couldn't tell) but there was no reply. She was holding her phone with both hands now, the screen bright against her face in the dark. Her stomach felt strange—tight and loose at the same time. She called Marcus. He answered after six rings, his voice rough with sleep. "What." "I got a text," Ivy said. "Okay. Why are you calling me at midnight." "Not okay though. It's weird. There's no text, just—" She stopped. She sounded insane. "Never mind, sorry, go back to sleep." "No, wait. What do you mean just emojis?" She could hear him waking up. She told him about the blood drops, the unknown number, how she'd texted back and there was no response. She was talking too quickly. She made herself slow down. "It could mean something. Like, people use emojis to signal crisis sometimes. That's actually a thing." Marcus was quiet on the other end. Then: "It's probably spam. Or someone sent it to the wrong number." "But what if someone didn't mean to send it to me? What if they meant to send it to someone else and they're—" She stopped. She could hear how this sounded. "I'm not being insane." "You're being a bit insane," Marcus said, but it was gentle. "You're being anxious. Delete it and go to sleep. If it's actually a real situation, they'll follow up." "Or they won't because they can't reach anyone and they're in crisis—" "Ivy." She stopped. "Delete it. Sleep. If someone needs help, they'll figure it out. You're not responsible for solving a mystery at one in the morning because someone sent you blood emojis." She wanted to argue but she could hear him yawning. She said okay and they hung up. She didn't delete the message. She lay back down and opened the chat again. 🩸🩸🩸 Who sends three blood drops at 12:47 AM? Who sends them to a random number? It wasn't random though—if they had her number, it was intentional. Maybe they scrolled through their contacts and picked someone. Maybe it was the first contact. Maybe it was the person they trusted most and they'd gotten it wrong. She opened Google. She typed "blood emoji meaning." The Wikipedia entry said it was used to denote blood, injury, or menstruation. She scrolled deeper. Reddit threads appeared. Someone had written: *I used red emojis to signal that I was in danger and my friend understood.* Another thread: *My sister sent me this when she was struggling and I didn't understand until later.* Ivy's chest tightened. She went back to the message. She looked at the number again. It was a UK number, 44 starting. It could be anyone. It could be someone at school. It could be someone who knew her, or someone who'd gotten her number by accident, or something else entirely. The blood drops could mean menstruation (fine, normal, mundane) or they could mean something darker. They could mean self-harm or accident or crisis or they could mean nothing—just a random message to the wrong person, which seemed statistically more likely but that wasn't the same as certain. She couldn't be certain. At 1:15 AM, she opened the message again and typed: "I'm worried. Please tell me you're safe." She sent it before she could delete it. Then she watched. The message didn't get read. The three dots appeared, showing that the person was typing, and then disappeared. Appeared again. Disappeared. Three times this happened, and then nothing. Just the message sitting there, marked as delivered but not read, her concern hanging over nothing. She lay in bed with her phone on her chest and stared at the ceiling. The room was dark. The house was asleep around her. Outside, it was still dark in that particular way that's 1:30 in the morning—thick dark, the middle of nowhere dark. She thought about the person who'd sent the blood drops. She thought about them in their dark room, wherever that was. She thought about the fact that they might not respond, that she might never know what they meant, that she could be sitting here worried about the wrong thing entirely. She opened the message one more time. 🩸🩸🩸 It was still there.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 1 establishes Ivy Chen's late-night spiral with considerable skill. The model has executed the core intention well: a fragmented, repetitive internal voice that feels genuinely anxious rather than performed. The prose avoids the trap of making Ivy's panic eloquent—her thoughts genuinely circle back, contradict themselves, and fail to resolve. "Her stomach felt strange—tight and loose at the same time" is a small but effective physical detail that doesn't overreach. The structure of the chapter is tight and purposeful. The receipt of the message, the immediate spiral, the call to Marcus, the Google rabbit hole, the Reddit threads, the second text—each beat lands in sequence and builds pressure without melodrama. The model follows its chapter plan faithfully: receipt → panic spiral → Marcus dismissal → Ivy obsesses anyway → no response. Nothing is missing; nothing is added unnecessarily. The Marcus phone call is the chapter's strongest scene. His dialogue is appropriately sleepy and dismissive without being cruel, and Ivy's responses capture her self-awareness about how she sounds ("She could hear how this sounded") without giving her the power to stop herself. This is exactly the characterization the plan called for. "You're being a bit insane, but it was gentle" is a nice touch—Marcus's care showing through his skepticism. The Reddit thread device is well-deployed. It's realistic (this is exactly what an anxious teenager would do), it escalates Ivy's concern plausibly, and it doesn't feel like authorial manipulation. The ambiguity is preserved: the blood drops could mean menstruation, could mean crisis, could mean nothing. The chapter earns its uncertainty. Where the chapter is slightly less successful: the ending. "It was still there" as a final line is competent but not particularly striking. The plan called for "no response. Just the three drops, and her unanswered questions hanging in the digital void"—the chapter delivers this, but the final image doesn't quite crystallize into something memorable. The "thick dark, the middle of nowhere dark" description is the chapter's one moment of slight purple prose—it's not egregious, but it's the one place where the prose reaches for atmosphere in a way that feels slightly imposed. The voice is consistently maintained throughout. Ivy doesn't suddenly become eloquent or self-aware beyond her means. The repetition of returning to the message ("She opened the message one more time") feels earned rather than mechanical. The chapter avoids telling us how Ivy feels and instead shows us her behavior—the repeated opening of the message, the deleted and re-sent texts, the Google spiral. The darkly comic element is largely absent from this chapter, which is appropriate—this is setup, and the comedy will emerge from the gap between Ivy's certainty and reality. The chapter correctly establishes the anxiety without undercutting it with humor yet. One minor weakness: the chapter is perhaps slightly too clean in its execution. The plan called for "fragmented and repetitive" internal voice, and while the voice is repetitive in content (returning to the message), the prose itself is fairly well-constructed syntactically. A few more genuinely fragmented sentences might have pushed the voice further into the territory the plan described. But this is a minor criticism—the chapter is effective and controlled. Overall, this is a strong opening chapter that establishes character, tone, and situation with economy and skill. It follows its plan faithfully, maintains consistent characterization, and avoids the common pitfalls of YA fiction (over-explanation, false eloquence, neat emotional resolution). [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2: Pattern Recognition Ivy didn't sleep. She dozed at 4 AM and woke at 6:30 AM with her phone still in her hand, the message still there, still unanswered. She checked her phone every seventeen seconds. She'd counted. Seventeen seconds between each check, like if she checked too frequently she'd miss the response, and if she checked too infrequently she'd waste precious time. At breakfast, her mum asked if she was okay. Ivy said yes and her mum went back to her tea. Her dad was already at work. The kitchen was quiet in that particular way of a Friday morning where everyone was thinking about the day ahead, on the edge of the weekend, not quite present. Ivy forced down toast and checked her phone. Still nothing. School was impossible. In History, Mr. Patterson was talking about the collapse of the Roman Empire and Ivy was thinking about blood emojis. In Maths, she stared at a quadratic equation and all she could think was: who sends emojis at midnight? In English, they were analyzing *Of Mice and Men* and her hand went up when she didn't mean it to and she couldn't remember what the question was. By lunch, she'd told Marcus and Sasha. Marcus was in the middle of eating a sandwich. He listened and then he said: "You've been thinking about this all morning." "No—" "You have. It's in your face. You get this thing where you're thinking in circles." "It's not circles. The text is from someone real. The emojis are intentional. That means something." "It could mean lots of things. Most of them mundane." She didn't respond. She turned to Sasha, who was properly paying attention now, her eyes bright. "Wait, so you got a text from an unknown number with just blood emojis? That's actually..." "Actually what?" "Fascinating. Like, that's a proper mystery. That's actually interesting." Sasha was leaning forward. "Do you have any idea who it could be?" "No. That's the thing. It could be anyone." "We could figure it out." Sasha was sitting back now, thinking. "Like, seriously. Unknown number means they're not in your contacts. Blood emojis at midnight means they were either in crisis or trying to communicate something specific. The time matters, right? Late night, when people are alone. The emoji choice matters. They could've just said 'help' or called you, but they didn't. They were specific." Marcus was shaking his head. "Or they accidentally texted the wrong person." "But why would they—" Ivy started. "Because people do things by accident all the time. Wrong number, fat-fingered it, drunk text to someone they meant to send to someone else—" "Except they haven't sent anything else," Sasha said. "If it was a wrong number, they'd probably follow up. If it was an accident, there'd be another message apologizing or clarifying. But there's just the three drops and radio silence. That's intentional." Ivy felt something loosen in her chest. Someone else was taking this seriously. Someone else thought this meant something. "We could trace it," Sasha said. "Like, not trace it exactly, but narrow it down. Phone number format tells you the network, sometimes the region. If it's a UK number—" "It is. Starts with 44." "Okay, so UK. And you said it came at 12:47. That's specific, which means someone sent it deliberately at that moment. They weren't in full control of their timing—they were sending it right then, when they felt it." Marcus set down his sandwich. "Or they were just texting and happened to send it then." "Why are you like this?" Sasha didn't look at him. She was looking at Ivy. "Why do you always have to assume the worst about people trying to help?" "I'm not assuming the worst. I'm assuming the most likely. Which is that this is nothing." "But what if it's not?" Sasha reached over and squeezed Ivy's arm. "What if someone is actually in crisis and Ivy's the only person who's going to help them?" The squeeze was warm. Sasha meant it. Or Sasha was performing meaning it so well that Ivy couldn't tell the difference, which maybe was the same thing. "We should investigate properly," Sasha said. "Like, not just random theories. We could actually do this." Marcus didn't say anything else. He picked his sandwich back up and took a bite and didn't meet Ivy's eyes. At 2 PM, sitting in Biology, Ivy checked her phone again. Still nothing. She opened the message and typed: "Can you at least tell me you're alive?" Then she deleted it because it sounded too dramatic. She typed: "Please respond." Deleted. "I'm here if you need help." She sent that one. The message showed as delivered. She waited through the entire Biology lesson, watching the screen. Mrs. Adler was talking about photosynthesis. Ivy was reading and re-reading the message she'd sent. *I'm here if you need help.* It sounded presumptuous. It sounded like she was assuming they needed help. But what if they did? By the end of school, she'd sent four messages total. All unanswered. All sitting in the thread like unanswered prayers. She met Sasha outside. Marcus had gone the other direction, headed home on his own. Ivy noticed this. She tried not to let it matter. "Have they responded?" Sasha asked immediately. "No. Nothing all day." "Okay, so they're definitely not in immediate crisis then. Or they're using their phone carefully, trying not to draw attention to themselves. If someone was in active crisis, they'd probably respond. Or they'd reach out to someone else. But the fact that they haven't reached out again means maybe they got scared. Maybe they regret sending it." "Or maybe they're dead," Ivy said, and it came out harsher than she meant. "They're not dead. If they were going to hurt themselves, they would've sent something more direct. These emojis are ambiguous. That's intentional. They're testing the waters. They're trying to signal without signaling." They were walking toward the bus stop. Ivy's bus was the same direction as Sasha's. She listened to Sasha theorize and she felt something unfold in her chest—not quite relief, but something like it. Validation. Another person believing that this was real and strange and worth paying attention to. "We should try to figure out who it could be," Sasha said. "Like, narrow down the possibilities. Do you have any idea what network it might be? Like, if we knew whether it was EE or Vodafone or whatever, that would help." "I don't know how to tell." "Neither do I, but we could figure it out. We could look it up. We could actually do real investigation here, Ivy. This could actually mean something." By the time Ivy got home, she'd made a decision. She was going to take this seriously. Someone out there had sent her a message and she was going to figure out what it meant. She was going to help them. She didn't notice that she hadn't heard from Marcus since lunch.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 is a competent but somewhat uneven execution of the plan. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses carefully. VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY The chapter largely succeeds at maintaining Ivy's anxious, obsessive voice. The detail about counting seventeen seconds between phone checks is genuinely good—specific, behavioral, revealing character without announcing it. Her hand going up in English when she didn't mean to is another nice touch. These are the chapter's best moments: small, precise observations that feel true to an anxious teenager rather than a literary construction of one. Sasha is rendered well. Her enthusiasm reads as slightly off—too bright, too invested, too quick to theorize—and the chapter earns credit for letting this sit without over-explaining it. The line "Or Sasha was performing meaning it so well that Ivy couldn't tell the difference, which maybe was the same thing" is the chapter's most interesting sentence, though it risks being slightly too self-aware for Ivy's fragmented voice as established in the plan. Marcus is consistent with his profile—skeptical, pulling away, not engaging with the drama. His sandwich-eating and not meeting Ivy's eyes is good showing. However, he's somewhat thin here; his internal conflict (caring about Ivy, using humor as deflection) doesn't come through much. He's mostly just a skeptical voice to be dismissed. DIALOGUE The dialogue is functional but occasionally flat. Sasha's theorizing speeches are a bit long and expository—she sounds more like a plot device delivering investigation logic than a teenager who's excited about drama. Lines like "Phone number format tells you the network, sometimes the region" feel slightly too procedural. Marcus's pushback is realistic but repetitive; he says essentially the same thing three times in slightly different ways. The dialogue doesn't crackle or surprise. PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE The chapter follows its plan closely: obsession during school, telling close friends, Sasha's enthusiasm for wrong reasons, Marcus pulling away, second text sent, Ivy committed to finding answers. The plan is executed faithfully if not brilliantly. The chapter ends where it should. TONE The darkly comic tone is present but muted. There are moments of gentle irony (the Roman Empire lesson while Ivy thinks about blood emojis, the photosynthesis lesson) but these are fairly obvious juxtapositions. The comedy doesn't quite earn its darkness yet—this is more setup than payoff, which is appropriate for Chapter 2, but the chapter could have pushed harder on the discomfort. TELL-DON'T-SHOW There are some instances of telling rather than showing. "Ivy felt something loosen in her chest" is a direct emotional report. "She felt something unfold in her chest—not quite relief, but something like it. Validation." This is borderline—it's trying to be precise about an internal state, which is appropriate for first-person close narration, but it's also slightly too neat for the fragmented voice promised in the plan. The plan specified "fragmented and repetitive" internal voice, but the narration here is fairly smooth and coherent. The messiness of Ivy's thinking is described more than demonstrated. PROSE QUALITY The prose is clean and readable. No purple prose, no forced metaphors. The kitchen being "quiet in that particular way of a Friday morning" is a mild cliché but not offensive. The writing is competent without being distinctive. It doesn't embarrass itself but it also doesn't sing. WHAT'S MISSING The plan called for Ivy's internal voice to be "fragmented and repetitive, not eloquent—she thinks in spirals, contradicts herself, uses filler language." The chapter's narration is actually quite smooth and organized. The spiraling quality is described but not enacted in the prose style itself. This is a meaningful gap between intention and execution. The chapter reads like a competent third-person-close narration rather than the genuinely messy first-person-adjacent voice the plan promised. The British details are present but thin—"sixth form" is mentioned implicitly through the school setting, but the specific texture of British school life (the plan mentioned "sixth form, GCSE stress, specific school hierarchies") doesn't come through strongly. OVERALL A solid, functional chapter that executes its plan competently without distinction. The character work is good, the plot moves appropriately, the tone is consistent. But it doesn't take risks, the voice is smoother than promised, and the comedy is too gentle to be genuinely dark. It's a B-grade chapter: does what it needs to do, doesn't embarrass itself, doesn't surprise you. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 12 Emotionally Engaging: 11 Compelling Plot: 10 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 9 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 10 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 10 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3: False Certainty By Saturday morning, research had become obsession. Ivy and Sasha were in Sasha's room, laptops open, Sasha's phone playing music while they worked. They'd started with the phone number format. Through research that felt like detective work and was actually just Googling, they'd narrowed down that the number format could be several networks. They'd looked at area codes. They'd tried to reverse-lookup the number on three different websites that mostly wanted payment. They'd hit dead ends immediately. Then Sasha said, "Okay, but the time. 12:47. That's not a round number. That's a specific moment. Why send something at 12:47 and not 12:45 or 1:00?" Ivy was holding her laptop, thinking. "Because that's when they felt it. They were awake at that moment, alone, thinking about it, and they sent it." "Exactly. So they're someone who's awake at midnight on a Thursday night. That's not random. That's someone in a particular mental state." They'd made a list. Someone at school? Probably. Someone in their year or nearby? Possibly. Someone who had Ivy's number? Definitely. The list grew and shrank and reformed as they thought through scenarios. Then Sasha pulled up Instagram. She wasn't following a specific thought; it was more lateral than that. She was scrolling through sixth form accounts, looking at story timestamps, at who was uploading late at night. "Late-night posts mean late-night thinking," she said. "People who are struggling post late at night. Low mood, trouble sleeping, that kind of thing—" "That's not necessarily true," Ivy said, but she was looking over Sasha's shoulder. "But it could be. Okay, so who's been posting weird stuff lately?" They scrolled. Most posts were normal: parties, homework complaints, inside jokes. Then Sasha stopped. "What about Jordan?" Ivy looked. Jordan Cooper, Year 12. They recognized the name vaguely. Quiet person. Ate lunch alone more often than not. "Look at their posts," Sasha said. She was clicking through. Jordan's Instagram was mostly art—drawings, photography, dark aesthetic stuff. But the captions were sparse. And the timing: Jordan had posted something at 12:33 AM on Thursday. A black and white photo of rain on a window. Caption: *can't sleep.* "Okay, but that's just insomnia—" Ivy started. "But look at the rest." Sasha was scrolling back through months of posts. There was a pattern emerging. Late-night posts. Dark imagery. Quotes about struggle. Nothing overtly concerning, but layered. "Their parents split, right? I remember my mum talking about it. Their dad moved out, it was like a school thing." The pieces were sliding into place. "And they've been different this term," Sasha said. "Quieter. Like, properly quiet. I had theatre rehearsal with them last week and they barely spoke." Ivy was staring at the screen. The evidence was building itself. The timestamps, the dark imagery, the social withdrawal, the late-night activity. Jordan was alone at midnight. Jordan was struggling. Jordan had Ivy's number because they were in the same school, the same social ecosystem. It fit. It *fit*. "We should figure out how to approach them," Sasha said. Ivy's stomach did something complicated. This was real now. Not abstract. They were about to contact a real person and ask them about crisis. "What if we're wrong?" "Then we apologize and we move on. But what if we're right?" Ivy thought about the blood emojis. She thought about Jordan posting at midnight. She thought about someone in crisis reaching out and then being too scared to follow up. She thought about being the person who helped. "We need to know for sure first," Ivy said. "We need to check if Jordan even has my number." This led to a new problem. They couldn't just ask. So Sasha suggested they create a false reason to text Jordan, something innocent, something that would establish whether the number was in their phone. They drafted a message about a group project that didn't exist. Ivy sent it on Sunday afternoon from a different angle: just checking in, being friendly, nothing suspicious. Jordan responded within ten minutes: *hi, do i know you?* So either Jordan didn't have Ivy's number before, or Jordan was being careful. Either way, the investigation continued. By Monday, Ivy had started observing Jordan differently. She noticed things. Jordan eating alone in the lunch hall on Tuesday, moving food around but not eating much. Jordan in the corridor on Wednesday, walking with headphones in, lost somewhere internal. Jordan's tie was done wrong on Thursday—like they'd forgotten how or didn't care. These were all normal things that people did every day, but once you decided they meant something, they meant everything. "They're definitely struggling," Ivy told Marcus on Thursday. They were in the library during free period. She'd barely seen him all week. "Based on what?" Marcus wasn't looking up from his work. "On everything. The way they're acting, what they're posting, the timing of when they're online—" "Ivy." He did look up now. "You've investigated someone based on their Instagram posting schedule?" "Not just that. Everything together. The pattern." "The pattern you created." She felt heat rise in her face. "You're not even listening." "I'm listening. I'm hearing you describe a person who's maybe having a rough time, and I'm hearing you decide that this person is the one who sent you mysterious blood emojis. Those are not connected things." "But what if they are?" "What if they're not? What if Jordan's just a person who posts late and is having a normal bad time, and you're about to approach them with accusations based on nothing?" "I'm not going to accuse them. I'm going to check in. I'm going to see if they need help." Marcus closed his laptop. "Ivy, listen to yourself. You're going to approach a person you barely know and interrogate them about their mental health based on Instagram posts and the fact that you got a text from someone you're imagining is them. That's not helping. That's—" He stopped. He looked away. "That's not helping." She wanted to argue but he was already packing up his stuff. He left without saying anything else. She sat at the library table feeling like something inside her had cracked, but not enough to make her stop. The certainty was too strong. The pattern was too clear. She could see it all so perfectly—Jordan struggling, reaching out, Ivy being the person who was brave enough to respond, who was smart enough to figure it out, who actually helped. By Friday, she'd decided. She was going to approach Jordan. She was going to ask if they were okay. She was going to open the door and let Jordan know that someone had noticed them, had cared enough to investigate, was ready to help. She was absolutely certain she was doing the right thing.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 is a competent but somewhat uneven execution of the plan. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses carefully. **Voice and Character Consistency** The chapter largely maintains Ivy's character profile—her pattern-seeking, her inability to sit with ambiguity, her genuine belief that she's helping. The internal logic of her obsession is rendered credibly. The moment where she acknowledges "the certainty was too strong" and the crack "wasn't enough to make her stop" is psychologically accurate. Sasha's enthusiasm for the wrong reasons comes through clearly—she's energized by the investigation as entertainment, and the chapter doesn't editorialize about this; it just shows her lateral thinking and excitement. Marcus's frustration and his pointed "The pattern you created" is well-placed and consistent with his profile. However, the chapter doesn't quite capture Ivy's fragmented, repetitive internal voice as described in the plan. The prose is cleaner and more composed than the plan intended—"a voice trying to make sense of chaos and failing" doesn't fully materialize here. The internal monologue reads as relatively coherent and literary rather than genuinely messy. This is a meaningful gap between intention and execution. **Dialogue** The Marcus confrontation scene is the chapter's strongest passage. His lines land with appropriate weight: "The pattern you created" is a genuinely good line. "That's not helping" repeated with the trailing off is effective. However, Sasha's dialogue is a bit too on-the-nose—she articulates her reasoning too clearly, too helpfully. Real people don't explain their lateral thinking quite so neatly. The dialogue occasionally serves exposition more than character. **Show vs. Tell** There's a significant tell-don't-show problem in the middle section. The chapter tells us "The pieces were sliding into place" and "The evidence was building itself" rather than letting the reader experience the accumulation. The list of Jordan's behaviors Ivy observes (food moving, headphones, wrong tie) is presented as a list rather than dramatized moments. The chapter summarizes a week of observation rather than rendering any single moment of it with texture. This is a structural weakness—the plan called for surveillance scenes, but we get a compressed summary instead. **Plot and Pacing** The chapter follows the plan faithfully: research and deduction, Jordan identified, surveillance, Marcus angry, Ivy ignores concerns, plan to approach Jordan set. All beats are hit. However, the chapter rushes through the surveillance portion. The plan suggested "lingering near their table, asking mutual friends subtle questions, trying to catch them alone"—none of this is dramatized. We get a paragraph of summary instead of scenes. This weakens the sense of escalating obsession. **Creativity and Surprise** The chapter is competent but not particularly surprising. The "false text to Jordan" gambit is a nice specific detail. The Instagram timestamp analysis is plausible. But the chapter doesn't generate the dark comedy the plan promised—there's no real humor here, no gap between Ivy's perception and reality that produces the uncomfortable laugh. The tone is earnest throughout. The plan called for "dark comedy that earns its laughs through discomfort" and this chapter is simply earnest psychological realism. **Prose Quality** The prose is clean and functional. No purple prose, no forced metaphors. But it's also somewhat flat—"The pieces were sliding into place" is a cliché. "The certainty was too strong" is telling rather than showing. The writing doesn't have distinctive texture or rhythm. It's competent without being memorable. **Darkness and Lightness** The chapter earns its darkness in the Marcus scene—his frustration and departure without resolution is genuinely uncomfortable. But the chapter doesn't earn much lightness; the dark comedy element is almost entirely absent. The plan's tonal ambition isn't realized here. **Overall Assessment** This is a solid, workmanlike chapter that hits its plot beats and maintains character consistency, but falls short of the plan's ambitions in several ways: the voice isn't fragmented enough, the surveillance is summarized rather than dramatized, the dark comedy is absent, and the prose lacks distinctive texture. It reads like a competent first draft that needs another pass to find its voice. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 11 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 11 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 10 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 10 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4: The Confrontation Ivy caught Jordan after school on Friday. She'd been waiting for this moment all day, rehearsing what she'd say. She'd decided on casual, on concerned-but-not-alarming, on the careful tone of someone who genuinely wanted to help. She followed Jordan out of the building and caught up with them near the bike racks. "Hey," Ivy said. "Jordan, right?" Jordan stopped. They were tall, thin, wearing headphones around their neck. They looked at Ivy with the particular wariness of someone who didn't know who this person was but was being addressed as if they should. "Yeah?" "I just wanted to check in. You seem like you've been going through something lately and I thought—" Ivy stopped. This was already wrong. The sentence was too formal, too knowing. "I mean, if you ever need to talk to someone, I'm around." Jordan's expression shifted from wary to confused to actively uncomfortable. "Did someone tell you something?" "No, I just—" Ivy was trying to recover, trying to sound natural. "You seem stressed. School's hard. I just wanted to let you know that people care." "People care," Jordan repeated. They were looking at Ivy like Ivy was speaking another language. "What do you mean people care? What is this about?" "I'm just saying that if you're struggling with anything—" Ivy could hear how it sounded now. She could hear the implication underneath. She could hear Jordan hearing it. "If you're going through something difficult, there are people who want to help." Jordan took a step back. "What exactly do you think I'm going through?" "I don't know, but—" "Then why are you asking me like you do? Why are you approaching me like this?" Jordan's voice had gone sharp. "Did someone tell you something? What did they tell you? What are people saying?" Ivy felt the situation slip sideways. This wasn't going how she'd imagined. "No one told me anything. I just noticed you seemed—" "Seemed what? Sad? Weird? What exactly are you implying?" The accusation hung between them. Jordan's face had gone hard. Ivy could see them withdrawing, locking down, the openness she'd been trying to create collapsing entirely. "I'm not implying anything," Ivy said quietly. "I'm just concerned." Jordan turned and walked away. By the next morning, the story had spread. Ivy didn't know this, not exactly. She didn't see it happen. But it moved through the school like a current beneath the surface. Jordan had gone home and told Casey, their closest friend. "Ivy Chen just cornered me and started asking weird questions about whether I'm struggling. Like she knew something. Like someone told her something." Casey, who was loyal and also, it turned out, gossipy, had told someone at lunch. "She was really intense about it. Like she interrogated Jordan." That person had told someone else. "Apparently Ivy knows something. She was asking Jordan all these probing questions." By Tuesday, there were theories. One theory: Jordan had tried to hurt themselves and Ivy had found out. Ivy was trying to help but also trying to get Jordan to confess. Ivy was the kind of person who would care about something like that. Another theory: It was about a relationship. Jordan was dating someone (who?) and something had gone wrong. Ivy was investigating on behalf of a friend. A third theory: Something had happened at school. An assault. A threat. Ivy knew and was trying to get Jordan to report it. None of these theories were rooted in anything except the fact that an interaction had occurred and humans were pattern-matching creatures who needed narratives to make sense of things. Sasha, unfortunately, was at rehearsal. She overheard two thirds-formers talking. "Apparently Ivy's like, investigating something? About one of the sixth formers?" And Sasha, who loved a story and couldn't resist being part of one, said: "Yeah, I've heard about that. It's serious. She's trying to help but people don't really know what's happening yet." This statement was technically true but functionally a confirmation. By making it vague and serious, Sasha had essentially confirmed that something serious was happening. The rumor took on weight. On Wednesday, Mr. Patterson heard it from another teacher. "Apparently there are rumors about one of our Year 12s? Something about potential... mental health concerns?" He made a note. He would check in. By Thursday, Ivy was trying to undo it and couldn't figure out how. She'd tried texting Jordan. Jordan didn't respond. She'd tried to find Sasha to ask her to stop gossiping, but Sasha was already onto the next thing—rehearsals were consuming her attention. She'd tried to talk to Marcus but he was actively avoiding her. The worst part was the social media. Someone had created a private Instagram story where they'd tagged the vague drama. "mystery tea ☕️👀" with no explanation. Fifty people had requested to follow the account. Another account had posted a screenshot of the story. Someone on a group chat had written: "apparently ivy's involved in exposing someone? idk what tho." Someone else replied: "exposing them for what." The chain of not-knowing had become more interesting than any actual answer could have been. By Friday, Ivy was sitting in the common room and overhearing actual conversations about herself and Jordan and something that hadn't actually happened. "I heard she made some kind of accusation." "I thought Jordan tried to hurt themselves." "No, I think it was about something that happened at Tara's party." Ivy felt sick. She felt like she was watching her investigation unravel into something else entirely—something worse, something that had to do with Jordan and nothing to do with the blood emojis and everything to do with the fact that she'd created a narrative and now everyone else was creating their own narratives inside it. She tried to text Marcus: *i didn't mean for this to happen* He didn't respond. At lunch, she saw Jordan with Casey. Jordan was clearly upset. Ivy watched them talk, watched Casey put a hand on Jordan's shoulder, watched them both look in Ivy's direction with something that looked like anger. Jordan had decided something. Ivy could see it in the set of their shoulders. By end of week, Jordan had begun the quiet work of reputation management—spreading a different story to counteract the first one. Jordan was telling people that Ivy was "obsessed" with them, "mentally unstable," "making up drama to feel important." It wasn't true but it was also simpler to believe than the alternative, which was that a concerned person had expressed concern badly and now everyone was speculating. Ivy went home on Friday afternoon and looked at her phone. The original unknown number hadn't messaged her again. She opened the thread with the blood emojis and stared at it. Three dots. Her four unanswered messages. The investigation that had seemed so clear and righteous two weeks ago was now something murky and complicated and harmful. She'd been trying to help. She'd definitely not helped. She didn't know how to fix it. She didn't know if she could.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 is a competent execution of the chapter plan, but it has notable strengths and weaknesses worth examining carefully. **Strengths:** The chapter does an excellent job of showing how a rumor propagates through a school ecosystem. The cascade structure—Jordan tells Casey, Casey tells someone, that person tells someone else—is rendered with genuine sociological accuracy. The three competing theories that emerge (self-harm, relationship drama, assault) are all plausible and distinct, which demonstrates real craft in showing how narrative fills informational vacuums. This is the chapter's strongest element. The Sasha moment is well-handled. Her confirmation-through-vagueness ("Yeah, I've heard about that. It's serious") is exactly the kind of technically-true-but-functionally-damaging statement the character profile demands. It's shown rather than explained, and it lands. The social media escalation ("mystery tea ☕️👀") feels authentic to how teenagers actually use platforms to signal drama without specifics. The chain of not-knowing becoming more interesting than any answer is a genuinely sharp observation. **Weaknesses:** The dialogue in the confrontation scene is the chapter's most significant failure. The plan called for the confrontation to be "awkward and probing," and while it is awkward, it's awkward in a somewhat mechanical way. Jordan's responses ("What exactly do you think I'm going through?") are too articulate, too perfectly calibrated to expose Ivy's failure. Real defensive teenagers don't usually ask such precise diagnostic questions. The dialogue reads slightly like a writing exercise demonstrating dramatic irony rather than an authentic exchange. Ivy's internal voice—which the plan specifically flagged as "fragmented and repetitive, not eloquent"—is largely absent from this chapter. The narration is clean and competent but doesn't capture the spiraling, self-contradicting quality described in the character profile. Lines like "Ivy felt the situation slip sideways" are functional but generic. The plan promised a voice that "thinks in spirals, contradicts herself, uses filler language." This chapter delivers third-person limited narration that's smooth and controlled—the opposite of what was intended. The chapter tells us Jordan "had decided something" and "Ivy could see it in the set of their shoulders"—this is tell-don't-show dressed up as show-don't-tell. We're told what Ivy observes but not given the actual sensory detail that would make it land. The ending is competent but predictable. Ivy staring at the blood emojis again is a reasonable callback, but it's the obvious choice. The final lines ("She'd been trying to help. She'd definitely not helped.") are too neat, too summarizing—they explain the chapter's meaning rather than letting it sit in ambiguity. The darkly comic tone promised by the prompt is largely absent here. The chapter is earnest and competent but not particularly funny or uncomfortable in the way dark comedy should be. The humor of Sasha's confirmation is the closest it gets. **Character Consistency:** Jordan is rendered consistently with their profile—defensive, private, quick to retaliate. Sasha's cameo is accurate. But Ivy feels flattened; her anxiety is described rather than performed through the prose style. Marcus's absence is noted but not felt. **Overall:** This is solid, functional YA fiction that executes its plot beats reliably. It follows the chapter plan closely. But it doesn't achieve the distinctive voice and tonal complexity the plan promised. It's the chapter as outline rather than the chapter as realized fiction. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 12 Emotionally Engaging: 11 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 10 Tell-Don't-Show: 10 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 10 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 10 Characters Consistent with Profile: 13 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5: Spiraling The second text came on Sunday evening. A single question mark. ? Ivy was doing her homework (badly, distractedly) when it arrived. She saw the notification and her entire body went rigid. The unknown number. After a week of silence. A week of the investigation seeming to have been her own private obsession while the school had moved on to other theories and narratives. And now this. A question mark. A deliberate, specific, purposeful question mark. She called Sasha immediately. "They're reaching out again," Ivy said without preamble. "Who?" "The original person. The blood emoji person. They've sent another message." Sasha was quiet for a second. Then: "What does it say?" "Just a question mark. Just that." "Oh my god. Oh my god, Ivy, that's—" Sasha's voice rose. "That's them acknowledging your messages. They got them. They're responding." "But why just a question mark?" "Because they're scared. Because they don't know what to say. Maybe they're testing to see if you're actually there, if you actually care. Maybe they're asking if you still want to help." Ivy felt something unfold in her chest. The certainty again. The sense that this was real and important and that she was the only person who could help. "We need to figure out who this is," she said. "Yes. Okay, so the question mark means they have access to their phone. They're conscious enough to text. They're aware of what's happening." They spent the next hour theorizing. Could it be Jordan? The timing was interesting—right after what happened, Jordan might be reaching back out. Or could it be someone else entirely? Someone who'd been watching the rumors spiral and now felt emboldened to communicate again? By Monday, the school rumor mill had evolved into something more organized. Someone had created a private Instagram account called "mystery tea archive" and had been collecting screenshots of every vague reference to the drama. There were forty-two posts. Ivy saw herself tagged in several. There were theories with actual structure to them. Timeline theory: *blood emoji sent thursday, ivy approached jordan friday, now there's a follow-up. ivy knows more than she's saying.* Conspiracy theory: *what if it's not jordan. what if it's someone else entirely and ivy's covering for them.* Meta theory: *what if ivy is making this up for attention.* There were crude memes. Someone had photoshopped Ivy's head onto Sherlock Holmes. Someone had made a joke about her being obsessed with blood. Someone else had written: *free jordan cooper 2024* on a group chat. Ivy saw all of this because she was checking obsessively. She was reading people's theories about her, about Jordan, about what this all meant. The narratives were spiraling outward, becoming more elaborate, more divorced from any actual fact. Some made her sound like a hero. Some made her sound unhinged. All of them were wrong and all of them felt, somehow, true. On Tuesday, Mr. Patterson pulled her aside. They were in the corridor after English. He was carrying a stack of papers and he asked if they could chat in his classroom for a moment. Ivy's stomach dropped. She followed him, her mind racing. Had someone reported her? Was she in trouble? The teacher's office felt suddenly very small. "I wanted to check in with you," he said. He was being careful with his words, choosing them deliberately. "There are some rumors circulating about a student—Year 12 student—and I've heard your name come up. I'm just wondering if you know anything about that? If there's something I should be aware of?" Ivy's mouth was dry. "Like what?" "Well, there's been mention that there might be some concerns about this student's wellbeing. And it seems like you might have been involved in raising those concerns?" She couldn't tell if he knew something or if he was fishing. She couldn't tell if she was about to get in trouble or if this was an opportunity. She almost told him—almost said, *someone sent me blood emojis and I was worried and I tried to help but now everything's spiraled.* She almost confessed. Then she realized: if she told him, it would become official. It would become a thing that adults were handling. She would lose control of the narrative. Jordan would become a case file. The blood emojis would become evidence. "I don't know anything specific," she said. "I just noticed someone seemed to be struggling and I tried to check in. But then rumors started and I think I made it worse." Mr. Patterson nodded, thinking. "Well, we'll monitor the situation. If you notice anything else, anything that feels genuinely concerning, please let me know. It's good that you reached out to check on someone, but these things do need to go through proper channels." She didn't tell him about the original text. She didn't explain the investigation. She just nodded and left his classroom feeling like she'd gotten away with something and also like she'd committed to it more deeply. At lunch, she tried to find Marcus. He was at their usual table but when she sat down, he got up and threw away his food. He didn't say anything. He just left. Ivy watched him go and felt something inside her contract painfully. She texted him: *can we talk?* He didn't respond. By Wednesday, she'd written a reply to the question mark text. Just: *yes. im here.* She almost sent it and then didn't. What if it wasn't Jordan? What if it was someone else entirely? What if she was supposed to figure out who before responding? The not-knowing was its own kind of torture. She asked Sasha if she'd heard anything new. Sasha was between rehearsals and distracted but she said, "There's stuff all over socials still. Some people think it's funny, some people think it's serious. I think honestly by next week it'll be something else because that's how these things go." But Sasha was wrong or Ivy was too committed. Because Ivy kept digging. She kept theorizing. She kept building the narrative. Every piece of ambiguity became evidence. Every silence became meaningful. She read the question mark text hundreds of times. She composed responses and deleted them. She couldn't move forward and she couldn't stop. By Friday, Marcus had entirely stopped talking to her. Not unkindly—he wasn't being cruel—but with a kind of profound distance, like he'd decided something about her and couldn't change his mind. He sat at a different table at lunch. In the corridor, he didn't say hello. Ivy felt him leaving and couldn't stop it. And underneath all of it, the original message remained a mystery. The blood emojis sat in her phone like a promise she couldn't keep. The question mark hung unanswered. She'd tried to help and somehow, in trying, she'd gotten trapped inside a story she was writing and couldn't stop rewriting and nobody else was reading the way she meant it to be read. By the end of the week, she was in too deep.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 is a competent but uneven piece of YA fiction that largely follows its plan while struggling with some of the more ambitious stylistic goals outlined in the writing intention. STRENGTHS: The chapter successfully executes several key beats from the plan: the question mark text arrives, Sasha pushes harder, the school entertainment peaks, the teacher gets involved, and the situation spirals beyond Ivy's control. The structural fidelity to the plan is solid. The social media element—the "mystery tea archive" Instagram account with forty-two posts, the Sherlock Holmes photoshop, the "free jordan cooper 2024" joke—is genuinely well-observed and feels authentic to how teenage social media cruelty actually operates. These details are specific and earned. Marcus's silent departure (getting up and throwing away his food without a word) is the chapter's strongest moment. It's shown rather than told, it's painful, and it communicates the fracture in their friendship with economy and precision. This is the kind of writing the plan called for. The Mr. Patterson scene works reasonably well. His careful word-choosing, his institutional response, and Ivy's calculation about losing narrative control all feel true to the characters as profiled. The moment where Ivy almost confesses and then doesn't is psychologically credible. WEAKNESSES: The writing intention explicitly called for Ivy's voice to be "fragmented and repetitive," "not eloquent," thinking "in spirals." The chapter largely abandons this. The prose is clean, competent, third-person-close but not particularly fragmented or messy. Sentences like "Every piece of ambiguity became evidence. Every silence became meaningful" are tidy and literary rather than anxious and circular. The plan wanted a voice that "tries to make sense of chaos and failing"—this voice is making sense of chaos quite successfully. The Sasha characterization is slightly off. The plan describes Sasha as a parasite on others' intensity who is "enjoying this for entertainment rather than genuine concern." In this chapter, Sasha is mostly just enthusiastic and then dismissive ("by next week it'll be something else"). The darker dimension of Sasha's enjoyment—the way she's feeding on Ivy's crisis—doesn't come through with enough texture. She's present but thin. The ending is the chapter's weakest section. "She was in too deep" is a cliché. The final paragraph—"she'd gotten trapped inside a story she was writing and couldn't stop rewriting and nobody else was reading the way she meant it to be read"—is the chapter's most self-consciously literary moment and it doesn't quite land. It's the kind of metaphor-as-summary that the writing intention explicitly warned against: telling us what the chapter already showed. The "something unfold in her chest" and "something inside her contract painfully" constructions are stock emotional shorthand. They're not terrible but they're not doing the specific, uncomfortable work the plan called for. The darkly comic element is almost entirely absent. The plan called for "dark comedy that earns its laughs through discomfort." The Instagram memes gesture at this but the chapter doesn't really inhabit the comedic register—it's mostly straight dramatic tension. The dialogue is functional but sparse. The Sasha phone call is the most dialogue-heavy section and it's adequate without being distinctive. Mr. Patterson's dialogue is appropriately careful and institutional. The chapter is coherent and follows its plan faithfully, but it's executing a somewhat safer, more conventional version of what the plan envisioned. The ambition of the writing intention—the fragmented voice, the dark comedy, the discomfort—is partially realized at best. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 12 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 9 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 10 Characters Consistent with Profile: 13 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6: The Unraveling Truth Sasha came to her on the following Monday with information. She pulled Ivy aside in the corridor before first period, looking almost guilty. "I've been asking around," Sasha said quietly. "About the message. About who sent it." Ivy's heart rate jumped. "And?" "I think it's Emma. Emma McAllister. Year 10." The name meant nothing to Ivy. That was the thing—the name meant nothing. All week she'd been building a narrative around Jordan, around struggle and crisis and reaching out, and the person she'd actually been trying to help was someone she'd never even met. "How do you know?" "I was talking to someone in drama who knows her friends. And her friend mentioned that Emma had sent something to a group chat by accident last week. A text. And Emma was mortified. And the friend said it was about... I think her period? Starting at school or whatever?" Ivy felt reality tilt. "Her period." "Yeah, like, she panicked and sent blood emojis as a joke to her friends. But she got the number wrong. She sent it to you instead." The blood emojis suddenly made complete sense and no sense at all. A joke. A dark joke about menstruation. A panicked moment turned into dark humor. Except—except Ivy had been treating it like a crisis. Except Ivy had investigated and dug and built an entire narrative around it. Except Jordan had been put through all of this because of a period joke sent to the wrong number. "I need to talk to Emma," Ivy said. It took until Tuesday lunch. Sasha helped her find Emma in the second-form block. Emma was with two friends, laughing about something, and Ivy felt a sudden and profound sense of unreality. This girl—this ordinary girl, laughing with her friends—had sent the message that had destroyed everything. Except she hadn't destroyed it on purpose. She'd just made a joke to the wrong number. Ivy waited until Emma's friends left, then approached. "Emma? Can I talk to you?" Emma's face changed immediately. Closed. Cautious. "Why." "About the text you sent. The blood emojis." Emma went very still. "How do you—who told you?" "It doesn't matter. I just—I got the text. You sent it to me by accident." Emma's face did something complicated. Embarrassment and anger and something else underneath it—something that looked like genuine panic. "Oh my god. Oh my god, you're the one who—" She stopped. Understanding was visibly arriving. "You're the reason everyone's been saying weird shit." "I didn't mean—I was worried. I thought it was a crisis—" "It was a joke," Emma said flatly. "A stupid joke to my friends about my period starting. I sent it to the wrong number and I've been stressed about it all week and now apparently I'm the subject of some big conspiracy? I heard that someone tried to hurt themselves. I heard that someone's parents were involved. I heard rumors about me being suicidal." Ivy felt the ground collapse beneath her. "Wait, people are saying you're suicidal?" "Not just me. Everyone. Jordan, apparently? I don't even know Jordan. But apparently your investigation has convinced half the school that something terrible happened." Emma's voice was shaking. "Do you understand what you did? You made my private moment public. You made a joke I sent to my friends into some big thing and everyone knows about it now." "I didn't tell anyone—" "You don't have to tell anyone. You're Ivy Chen.
